steps.title: Match Caldwell's claim
steps.content: Caldwell says translating "mimesis" as "imitation" is too narrow because it suggests art merely copies existing reality. Support must show ancient Greek thinkers understood art's relation to reality in more varied ways, including possibilities beyond simple copying.

steps.title: Look for varied Greek views
steps.content: The supporting statement should contrast a copy-based view with a broader view. Evidence that Aristotle treated mimesis as art's ability to imagine possible conditions would directly challenge the idea that the term only means imitation and would show the multifaceted understanding Caldwell describes in ancient views.

steps.title: Reject weaker evidence
steps.content: A discusses the word's early usage, not the philosophical art-reality relationship. B names a shared concern with psychological effects, not a richer meaning of mimesis. C only gives chronology for Plato's use of the term. The answer is D.
